role_hints.role: Accountants and auditors (staff through senior manager, CPA-track)
role_hints.oews_basis: 13-2011 Accountants and Auditors 34.2% of wage bill (4-digit 5412 mix; within CPA offices proper this share is likely higher since siblings 541213/541214/541219 dilute it)
role_hints.note: Audit fieldwork, workpaper preparation, tie-outs, sampling, tax return preparation and technical memo drafting are heavily AI-exposed; professional-skepticism judgment calls, client walkthroughs and materiality decisions less so. The run should justify a within-role fraction from the fieldwork/judgment task split — plausibly 0.3-0.5.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-3031 Financial Managers 10.32% + 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 5.1%; note partner profit draws in LLPs sit largely outside QCEW wages
role_hints.approx_share_of_wage_bill: ~15% (of wages; true partner economics larger via draws)
role_hints.note: Audit opinions and return sign-off are licensure-gated; client relationships and pricing judgment are the durable human layer. Internal reporting and review workflow automates.

role_hints.role: Bookkeeping and client accounting services (CAS) staff
role_hints.note: Write-up, reconciliation and monthly close are near-fully automatable by platform-native AI — the fastest-compressing cost line.
Form-driven preparation automates; review and signature migrate up to CPAs.
The growing CAS/advisory arm; AI-augmented rather than replaced near-term.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-6014 Secretaries 2.02% + 43-9061 Office Clerks 1.44% + 43-3021 Billing Clerks 1.29% + 43-1011 Admin Supervisors 1.88%
role_hints.note: Scheduling, return assembly, e-file transmittal, engagement letters, billing.

role_hints.role: Long tail (IT, HR, other financial specialists, marketing)
role_hints.oews_basis: not in listed top-10; top-10 covers ~70% of the wage bill, est. remainder ~30%
role_hints.note: Mixed exposure; allocate rather than ignore — this tail is large enough to move the V total.

source_hints.source: Wolters Kluwer Annual Accounting Industry Survey
source_hints.why: Firm-level technology and AI adoption data across firm sizes.

source_hints.source: Thomson Reuters State of Tax Professionals Report (annual)
source_hints.why: AI adoption and pricing-model shift data among tax practices.
Trade-body primary data on member firm AI use.

source_hints.source: The Rosenberg National Survey of CPA Firm Statistics (annual)
source_hints.why: The benchmark on realization, rates, leverage and partner income — the evidence base for whether hourly billing passes AI savings through, and for normalized partner economics.
Management-of-accounting-practice benchmarks for small firms — fee structures, margins, billing models.

source_hints.source: AICPA Trends report (pipeline/supply of CPAs) and Rosenberg Survey succession sections
source_hints.why: Partner age distribution, succession-plan prevalence and the CPA pipeline shortage — both owners_60plus_pct and the labor-scarcity counterweight to deflation.

source_hints.source: Accounting Practice Sales and Poe Group Advisors published practice-sale data
source_hints.why: Small-practice multiples — beware: quoted as multiples of gross revenue with owner comp inside.

source_hints.source: Accounting Today Top 100 Firms and PE-deal coverage; Koltin Consulting commentary
source_hints.why: The post-2021 PE wave (EisnerAmper, Citrin Cooperman and followers) — consolidator counts, alternative-practice-structure deal terms and platform exit multiples. Use for B/M only.

capture_questions: Billing is a mix of hourly and fixed-fee compliance engagements with realization tracked against hours: when AI collapses fieldwork and prep hours, do competitive fee pressure and client fee-benchmarking pass the saving through as lower audit/tax fees, or do the CPA pipeline shortage and value-pricing adoption let firms hold price and keep the spread as margin? What does rate/realization evidence since 2023 actually show for small and mid-size firms?

Is audit/attest demand payer-mandated (lenders, bonding companies, regulators, grant agencies require a signed opinion) such that clients cannot bypass the firm no matter how capable AI gets — and what share of a typical small firm's revenue is attest vs. bypassable tax/CAS work?

For tax and client accounting services, do Intuit (TurboTax full-service, QuickBooks Live), IRS Direct File expansion and generic AI let SMB and consumer clients self-serve — where is the complexity/liability line above which they keep paying?

How much revenue is partner-relationship advisory versus commodity compliance, and does the relationship survive partner retirement and an acquisition — what do retention outcomes in the 2021-2025 PE deals show?

Does the alternative-practice-structure model (attest entity CPA-owned, ServiceCo investor-owned) hold up under state board and independence scrutiny, or is there regulatory risk to the consolidation structure itself?
terminal_value_question: Attestation is a legally required human signature — audit opinions, comfort letters and many state/federal filings must be signed by a licensed CPA firm, so a demand floor exists by statute. The terminal question is whether AI collapses the billable hours underneath faster than the CPA shortage and value pricing hold fees up: does audit and complex tax become a smaller-headcount stamp-and-judgment business with preserved or improved per-partner economics (operator cost cut), or does fee benchmarking hand the hour collapse to clients (product shrinkage)? Answer separately for attest, tax and CAS revenue lines — they have different floors, and only attest has the statutory one.
special_notes: Pricing-model deflation candidate: hourly and hours-anchored fixed fees convert AI efficiency into lower bills where clients benchmark fees; model pi_dist per revenue line (attest / tax / CAS-advisory) rather than blended, and cite realization trends.

Ownership/structure: state boards require CPA ownership of attest practices — PE deals use alternative practice structures (attest entity stays CPA-owned; non-attest ServiceCo investor-owned). The wave since 2021 is large and well documented; use it for B and M only (rule 2), and verify current deal pace rather than assuming 2021-2023 tempo.

EBITDA normalization: small-practice comps from brokers (Accounting Practice Sales, Poe Group) quote multiples of gross revenue with owner compensation inside — normalize to market-rate partner comp before comparing to PE platform EBITDA multiples; partner draws also sit outside QCEW wages, so the record's labor_share understates true labor cost at partnership firms.

The 150-hour rule and the CPA pipeline shortage currently support pricing — a documented counterweight to AI deflation; track whether states' 120-hour alternative pathways (in progress 2024-2026) relieve it.
Independence rules restrict cross-selling to attest clients — limits the consolidation synergy story; peer review and state board oversight apply to acquired practices.

Boundary confusion: the provided role_mix is 4-digit 5412, blending in tax prep chains (541213), payroll bureaus (541214) and bookkeeping firms (541219) — CPA-office shares of accountants are higher and of clerks lower than the listed mix; adjust and say so. Association data (AICPA, Rosenberg) covers CPA firms specifically and is the better anchor.
Seasonality: tax-season revenue concentration distorts quarterly staffing and any part-year financials; use full-fiscal-year figures.
